narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this. The admissions model evaluated your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 26, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 2 and 1 respectively, middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time. It predicted an 82% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model a significant dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Your LSAT score of 26, far under the dataset average of 36.70, was the second strongest driver toward predicting failure, as higher scores are strongly linked to bar success in the data. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, though below the 3.21 average, actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some resilience in your prior college work. Your Year 1 decile of 2 had a smaller push toward failure, while your full-time enrollment plan and middle income bracket offered minor boosts toward success, aligning with the majority of students who pass. Overall, your profile stands out as lower than typical admits in key academic metrics like deciles and LSAT, where most successful students cluster higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a standout reapplication. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing forward.
